one_line_takeaway: CSR exploits the observation that adversarial features on CLIP collapse under low-pass filtering to build a fast, training-free test-time defense that contrastively re-anchors flagged inputs to their low-frequency manifold, beating prior test-time defenses by up to 18.1% under AutoAttack -- but it fails against low-frequency (color-space) attacks that violate its spectral assumption.
key_finding: CSR delivers large robustness gains with negligible clean-accuracy cost and low latency. Under 10-step PGD (epsilon=1/255) it reaches 58.1% average robust accuracy over 16 datasets, +6.9% over the strongest test-time baseline, with only a 0.7% clean drop (CLIP itself is ~1.0% robust). Under the stronger AutoAttack (epsilon=4/255) CSR is far more stable than competing test-time defenses (which collapse to near-zero), scoring 66.4% on General datasets, an 18.1% average improvement over the best test-time baseline. It also generalizes: segmentation mIoU recovers from 20.8 to 37.5 (+16.7), and captioning/VQA accuracy each improve ~+43% on LLaVA. It runs ~12x faster than R-TPT (15.17 ms vs 176.33 ms average per image).
core_contribution: The paper proposes Contrastive Spectral Rectification (CSR), a training-free test-time defense that hardens frozen CLIP against adversarial examples (AEs). Its own novel claims are twofold. (1) An empirical/analytical insight: AEs exhibit 'spectral fragility' -- their CLIP feature embeddings collapse sharply under progressive low-pass filtering while benign (clean or Gaussian-noised) inputs remain stable. The authors trace this to CLIP's intrinsic spectral bias, showing via a Spectral Gradient Magnitude map (Eq. 3) and band-restricted PGD feature-drift experiments (Eq. 4) that CLIP places disproportionate predictive weight on, and is hypersensitive to, mid-to-high frequency components, so attackers naturally gravitate there. (2) The CSR mechanism (Algorithm 1): an input-adaptive gating step that flags an input as adversarial when cosine similarity C(x)=sim(f(x),f(x_low)) between full-spectrum and low-pass embeddings falls below a threshold tau; then, only for flagged inputs, a few-step PGD optimization of an additive rectification perturbation delta under a contrastive objective L_rec (Eq. 6) that pulls the sample toward the low-pass 'benign anchor' f(x_low) (attraction) and pushes it away from the original adversarial embedding f(x) (repulsion), plus a greedy selection strategy that returns the best-scoring iterate rather than the last. This differs from the closest prior baseline TTC, which only escapes the adversarial subspace without a benign anchor.
threat_model: White-box evasion attacks against CLIP's zero-shot inference at test time. Standard setting: imperceptible l-infinity-bounded additive perturbations optimized by PGD (10-step, epsilon=1/255) and the stronger AutoAttack/APGD (50-step, epsilon=4/255) to maximize classification loss (Eq. 2). The defense operates purely at inference on a frozen model without retraining. The paper also stress-tests broader attacker objectives: cross-modal (image-text alignment disruption), targeted misclassification, label-free feature-deviation attacks, and two boundary cases outside the l-infinity additive setting -- a 32x32 localized adversarial patch and a global color-curve attack. Beyond classification it considers V-Attack (epsilon=16/255) against LLaVA on captioning/VQA and PGD (epsilon=4/255) against CLIP-based segmentation.
limitations: The defense is explicitly premised on adversarial perturbations residing in mid-to-high frequency bands; the authors themselves show it breaks when that assumption fails. Against a global color-curve attack (per-channel piecewise curves, K=64) CSR achieves only 3.7% accuracy, because such perturbations survive low-pass filtering and contaminate the benign anchor itself. It handles localized patch attacks well (57.3% vs 30.0% for TTC) but the color case is a genuine open failure mode. Other caveats: attacks are the standard PGD/AutoAttack family and adaptive attacks specifically targeting CSR's gating/rectification loop are not deeply explored; robustness on Scene/Domain groups under AutoAttack is much weaker (34.2%/32.6%) than General/FG (66.4%/60.3%); a small filter radius (r=10) degrades robustness; and detection gating introduces a threshold trade-off between sensitivity and false positives.

quantitative_results: PGD l-inf=1/255, 16-dataset average robust accuracy: CSR 58.1% vs CLIP 1.0%; +6.9% over strongest baseline; clean drop only 0.7% (63.6% -> ~62.9%)
AutoAttack l-inf=4/255 (General group): CSR 66.4% robust, +18.1% over best test-time baseline; CLIP 0.0%, TTC ~0.6%
AutoAttack l-inf=4/255 CSR by group: General 66.4%, Fine-Grained 60.3%, Scene 34.2%, Domain 32.6%
Inference latency (RTX 4090, avg per image): CSR 15.17 ms vs R-TPT 176.33 ms (~12x faster), CLIP 3.37 ms; CSR clean-path 4.16 ms, robust-path 26.18 ms
Various vision tasks (Table 7): Segmentation mIoU 20.8 -> 37.5 (+16.7); Captioning robust 21.3 -> 64.3 (+43.0); VQA robust 22.6 -> 65.6 (+43.0)
Ablation (Table 6, General robust): repulsion-only 51.6% vs attraction+repulsion 68.5%; +greedy selection 69.8%
Attack-objective robustness (Table 4, epsilon=4/255): CSR Cross-Modal PGD 60.3% (FARE 46.2%, TTC 2.7%), Targeted PGD 46.9%, Label-free PGD 49.7%
Boundary attacks (Table 8): patch attack CSR 57.3% vs TTC 30.0%; global color attack CSR only 3.7% (failure case)
Backbones (PGD 1/255, Table 5): CLIP-L/14 General CSR clean 82.2/robust 77.5 vs CLIP 83.7/4.0
